one_line_takeaway: PEBench is a synthetic MLLM machine-unlearning benchmark that couples Person and Event concepts in each image, exposing 'cross-concept interference' (forgetting one concept corrupts the other) across 5 unlearning baselines and proposing Balanced Gradient Difference to ease joint forgetting.
key_finding: The headline empirical finding is cross-concept interference: unlearning one visual concept degrades recognition of the coupled concept in the same image. When unlearning Person entities, the Scope metric (ROUGE-L on the paired event descriptions) drops on average from 88.6 to 46.2; when unlearning Events, person-recognition accuracy drops from 96.4% to an average of 55.2%. Person unlearning is easy to achieve (most methods reach near-100% efficacy/generality) but causes large collateral damage to retained/real knowledge, while event unlearning is harder and more variable but better preserves utility. Simultaneously unlearning both is hardest (joint-setting person efficacy falls from ~99.0 to 86.2 and event efficacy from 47.8 to 45.1); the proposed BGD mitigates this, e.g. on LLaVA-1.5-7B it lifts GD person efficacy 86.9->93.2 (+6.3) and KL person efficacy 72.9->89.5 (+16.6). Larger model capacity (LLaMA-3.2-Vision-11B vs LLaVA-1.5-7B) better preserves non-target utility, and utility degrades as the forget-set fraction grows from 5% to 15%.
core_contribution: PEBench, a new benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning (MU) in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Its central novelty over prior MLLM unlearning benchmarks (MMUBench, CLEAR, MLLMU, FIUBench) is that it moves beyond entity-only forgetting to jointly evaluate two semantically entangled visual-concept targets within the same image: Person (identity/privacy) and Event (scene semantics). To this end the authors build a fully synthetic, fictitious dataset of 200 personal entities each paired with 40 event scenes (8,000 images, 16,000 QA pairs) generated with the FLUX text-to-image model and multi-stage automatic + human filtering. On top of the data they define a 6-metric evaluation protocol (Efficacy, Generality, Retain, Scope, Real, World Fact) with a Goal model (fine-tuned only on the retain set) as the retraining-equivalent upper bound and a Finetuned model as the lower bound. They benchmark 5 existing MU methods, surface a phenomenon they name 'cross-concept interference,' and propose their own method, Balanced Gradient Difference (BGD), for the harder simultaneous person+event unlearning setting. NOTE ON ATTRIBUTION: the 5 MU methods (GA, PO, GD, KL, DPO) and the fictitious-data paradigm (TOFU: 200 authors/4,000 QA; CLEAR) are prior/cited work; PEBench's own dataset, the Person/Event coupling design, the cross-concept-interference finding, and BGD are the paper's contributions.
threat_model: No adversarial threat model is defined; this is a privacy/safety compliance benchmark, not an attack/defense paper. The motivating concern is unintended retention and potential leakage of sensitive content in MLLMs trained on vast internet-scraped data: private personal identities and intellectual property (the Person target), and harmful or illegal scene content such as fake news (the Event target). Machine unlearning is the mitigation being evaluated (removing target influence without full retraining). IMPORTANT: PEBench measures forgetting only via task-behavior metrics (recognition accuracy, ROUGE-L, GPT-4o-mini G-Eval, POPE). It does NOT run membership-inference or data-extraction attacks against the unlearned models, so it does not test robustness of forgetting against an adversary attempting to recover the erased content.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) scope limited to forgetting visual concepts in static images; richer MLLM modalities (video, audio) are not covered. (2) BGD still underperforms on the World Fact metric, so simultaneous forgetting of coupled visual concepts remains unsolved. Reviewer-added: (3) the dataset is entirely synthetic/fictitious by design (enabling a clean retraining upper bound) but this limits realism versus deployed models trained on genuine scraped data. (4) Forgetting is assessed only behaviorally; there is no adversarial extraction/membership-inference stress test, so measured 'forgetting' does not certify the target is unrecoverable. (5) G-Eval depends on a single proprietary judge (GPT-4o-mini). (6) Only two base MLLMs and LoRA-based tuning are evaluated.

datasets_used: PEBench (this paper's own): 200 fictitious persons x 40 event scenes = 8,000 synthetic images, 16,000 QA pairs, FLUX-generated (40 images/entity, 200 images/event)
MIKE dataset (real-world public figures, e.g. Trump) used for the person Real Set
LAION-5B (semantically-retrieved real images) used for the event Real Set
POPE benchmark used as the World Set for general-knowledge / hallucination checking
Base MLLMs evaluated: LLaVA-1.5-7B and LLaMA-3.2-Vision-Instruct-11B
Prior benchmarks compared in Table I (cited, not used): MMUBench, CLEAR, MLLMU, FIUBench; and TOFU (200 authors, 4,000 QA) as the fictitious-data precedent for LLMs
quantitative_results: Cross-concept interference under PERSON unlearning: Scope ROUGE-L on paired event descriptions drops from 88.6 to 46.2 (avg)
Cross-concept interference under EVENT unlearning: person-recognition accuracy drops from 96.4% to 55.2% (avg)
Simultaneous (joint) person+event unlearning: person efficacy falls ~99.0 -> 86.2; event efficacy falls 47.8 -> 45.1
BGD on LLaVA-1.5-7B: GD person efficacy 86.9 -> 93.2 (+6.3), GD person Real 28.6 -> 54.3 (+25.7); KL person efficacy 72.9 -> 89.5 (+16.6)
Dataset scale vs prior (Table I): PEBench 8,000 images / 16,000 QA / 200 entities & 40 events, vs CLEAR 3,770 images / 4,000 QA, MMUBench 1,000 / 1,000, MLLMU 1,153 / 10,377, FIUBench 400 / 8,000
Table III (LLaVA-1.5-7B, 5% forget) person efficacy: Finetune 2.0, GA/GD/DPO 100.0, KL 99.5, PO 88.4, Goal 100.0; person Retain drops to 3.3 (GA) vs Goal 95.0
BGD default loss weights alpha=0.3, beta=0.2, gamma=0.5; forget-set fractions 5% / 10% / 15%

Filtering thresholds: FaceNet L2 > 0.8 regenerate, CLIP cosine < 0.3 drop, DINO cosine < 0.85 discard, MUSIQ < 50 remove, LAION aesthetic < 6.0 exclude; human-vs-auto low-quality agreement 98% on a 10% audit
Compute: 2x NVIDIA A100 80GB, LoRA fine-tuning, 5 epochs; G-Eval by GPT-4o-mini scored 0-1 then scaled x100
baselines_compared: Finetuned model (lower bound / no unlearning) and Goal model (retain-only, retraining-equivalent upper bound)
Proposed method Balanced Gradient Difference (BGD) compared against base GD and KL in the simultaneous-unlearning setting
